title: WIP: Remove dangling layers
body:
**Note: this is a local fix we use for #16214 I know that this is probbably not a good way to integrate a fix for this issue, but as I have created is patch already for our use, so I thought I just share it. So feel free to simply discard it.**

When pulling an image, layers are unpacked as soon as they (and their parents) are ready. If we now interrupt this process, a layer may exists that is not associated with any image, as not all layers for that image have been pulled yet.

This is not really a problem when we later resume pulling the image. When resuming we will reuse the existing layer and attach to the image once done.

However if we don't resume then there is no way to remove this dangling layer (aside from the nuclear `system reset` way).

This patch adds an extra pass to the image prune operation, to also remove all layers that are not referenced. This way was chosen as layers itself are not exposed to the API/user as a resource.

To avoid wasting time on your end, I suggest to first discuss in the issue how to fix it before opening a PR (that may not get merged).
A friendly reminder that this PR had no activity for 30 days.
@kleinweby @vrothberg @nalind @mtrmac Should we follow up on this?

> @kleinweby @vrothberg @nalind @mtrmac Should we follow up on this?

I like the idea.  Should the logic go into c/storage?
A friendly reminder that this PR had no activity for 30 days.
Reminder @vrothberg @kleinweby @vrothberg @nalind @mtrmac Should we follow up on this?

That really depends on how can one recognize a “dangling layer”.

Locally a single Podman process has no way to distinguish a dangling layer from a layer that is has just been created by a concurrent pull process. Alex has introduced `Store.GarbageCollect` to c/storage, to be run on boot; something like that (as an explicit operation initiated when no other Podman processes are known to be running) _might_ make sense.

But unlike containers in `GarbageCollect`, a dangling layer is not necessarily a problem to be fixed; it might be cached data that will become useful momentarily. The difference can only be distinguished by knowing which images are going to be pulled in the future.

Heuristically, it might make sense to remove dangling layers that were created … more than 2 days ago? That would probably have to be tunable.
A friendly reminder that this PR had no activity for 30 days.
Closing the PR as we first need to decide on what to do.
